QF.id: XIII Introduction to Forwards and Futures_60

question.text: What is the concept of 'cost of carry' being negative (inverse carry) and when does it occur?

answer.text: Negative cost of carry occurs when the income from holding the underlying (dividends for equity, coupon for bonds) exceeds the financing cost — resulting in futures trading below spot (backwardation)
Negative carry means the trader is losing money on their futures position
Negative carry is impossible — carry cost is always positive
Negative carry occurs only when interest rates are negative

explanation.text: **The Logic:** If dividends > risk-free rate (equity) or coupon > repo rate (bonds), the net carry cost becomes negative. Holder earns more from the asset than their financing cost. This means F < S (backwardation). Example: high-dividend stock in a low-rate environment. **The Trap:** Carry cost can be negative without negative interest rates. The concept applies to the NET carry (income minus financing), not just financing alone. **Cross-Exam Context:** Series VIII Ch3 covers negative carry for high-dividend stocks. Series IV discusses negative carry in inverted yield curve scenarios. Series XVI covers it through convenience yield exceeding storage costs. **Distributor's Pro-Tip:** Stocks with dividend yield > risk-free rate will have futures at a discount to spot — don't misinterpret this as bearish sentiment. It's a mathematical consequence of the carry model.
